Mr. Rakesh Kumar

System Analyst, Department of Computer Applications

Date: _______________

Abstract

EduNova is a cloud-hosted, full-stack IT Coaching Institute Management System designed to digitize and streamline the administrative and financial operations of a coaching institute. The system addresses key challenges faced by small and medium coaching centres — manual fee record keeping, lack of real-time visibility into student dues, handwritten receipts that cannot be reprinted, and the absence of structured financial reporting.

The system is built on a REST API backend developed using Python FastAPI, deployed on Amazon EC2 in the ap-south-1 (Mumbai) region. Data is persisted on Amazon DynamoDB using a single-table design with Global Secondary Indexes. The frontend is a Single Page Application (SPA) built with vanilla HTML5, CSS3, and JavaScript, distributed globally via Amazon CloudFront CDN.

Key modules include Student Management, Course and Fee Structure Management, Multi-Mode Fee Collection (Cash, Demand Draft, and Online via Razorpay), Dues and Defaulter Tracking, Reminder Scheduling, Financial Reports, and Role-Based User Management. The system generates professional, printable fee receipts after every payment and supports password reset via Twilio WhatsApp OTP. All API endpoints are secured using JWT Bearer token authentication.

Keywords: Coaching Institute Management, Fee Management System, FastAPI, AWS DynamoDB, Razorpay, CloudFront, JWT Authentication, MCA Project.

CHAPTER 1
Synopsis

1.1 Project Overview

EduNova is a modern, cloud-based IT Coaching Institute Management System developed to address the administrative and financial inefficiencies experienced by small and medium coaching centres. The system provides a comprehensive digital platform for managing student admissions, fee collection, course fee structures, dues tracking, defaulter management, reminder scheduling, and financial reporting — all accessible through a web browser from any device.

The system operates on a client-server architecture where the frontend Single Page Application communicates with the FastAPI backend via authenticated REST API calls. All data is stored on Amazon DynamoDB and the frontend is served through Amazon CloudFront CDN for fast, reliable global access.

1.2 Problem Statement

Coaching institutes typically rely on handwritten admission registers, manual fee receipt books, and spreadsheets to manage their day-to-day administrative tasks. This approach leads to critical operational problems: fee records are prone to human error, receipts cannot be reprinted once lost, there is no real-time view of which students have outstanding dues, and generating monthly financial reports requires hours of manual effort. Tracking student lifecycle stages — whether a student is studying, on break, has left, or has completed the course — is done informally, if at all. Existing software solutions are either too expensive for small institutes, require dedicated on-premise servers, or lack features specific to the coaching centre domain.

1.3 Proposed Solution

EduNova addresses these challenges through a cloud-hosted web application built on AWS infrastructure. The system eliminates the dependency on local servers and manual paperwork by providing a centralised, always-available platform that automates fee calculations, installment scheduling, receipt generation, and dues reporting. The application is accessible from any device with a browser, requires no installation, and is secured using JWT-based authentication with role-based access for admin and staff users.

1.4 Key Features

EduNova covers the complete student lifecycle from admission to passout. The student management module supports adding, editing, and searching student records with complete profile and fee history. The fee collection module supports Cash, Demand Draft, and Online via Razorpay payment modes with automatic receipt generation. The dues module provides real-time visibility into all students with outstanding balances. The defaulters module automatically flags students overdue by one or more months and escalates chronic cases after three months. A reminder scheduling module allows staff to set follow-up dates with promise amounts. The reports module provides monthly collection summaries, course-wise revenue breakdowns, and year-wise admission trends. The clearance module generates printable clearance certificates for students who have settled all dues. Password reset is handled via WhatsApp OTP using Twilio integration.

1.5 Technology Stack Summary

Table 1.1: Technology Stack Summary

LayerTechnology
FrontendVanilla JavaScript SPA, HTML5, CSS3
BackendPython 3.11, FastAPI (REST API Framework)
DatabaseAmazon DynamoDB (NoSQL, single-table design)
HostingAmazon EC2 — t2.micro, ap-south-1 (Mumbai)
CDNAmazon CloudFront (global content delivery)
PaymentsRazorpay Payment Gateway (test + live mode)
OTP / AuthTwilio WhatsApp API, JWT Bearer Tokens
Process Managersystemd service (auto-restart on reboot)
[ Figure 1.1 — System Architecture Overview ]
Insert architecture diagram here

Figure 1.1: System Architecture Overview

CHAPTER 2
Introduction to the Research Problem

2.1 Background

Coaching institutes form a critical segment of the Indian education ecosystem, providing specialized instruction in computer science, professional courses, and vocational skills. India has over 1.5 million private coaching centres, the vast majority of which are small enterprises employing fewer than 20 staff members. Despite the scale of the sector, most institutes continue to operate using manual or semi-manual administrative systems.

The administrative workload of a coaching centre includes student admission processing, course enrollment, monthly fee collection, due tracking, attendance management, certificate issuance, and financial reporting. When managed manually, these tasks consume a disproportionate share of staff time, introduce errors, and offer no consolidated view of the institute's financial health.

2.2 The Digital Transformation Imperative

The widespread availability of cloud computing, affordable internet connectivity, and digital payment infrastructure has created a clear opportunity for coaching institutes to modernize their operations. Cloud-based systems offer accessibility from any device at any time, automatic data backup with no risk of physical loss, real-time data synchronization across all users, and the ability to scale without additional hardware investment. Despite these advantages, adoption of cloud-based management software remains low among small coaching centres, primarily due to cost concerns, lack of awareness, and the absence of affordable solutions tailored to their specific operational workflows.

[ Figure 2.1 — Manual vs Cloud-Based Institute Management ]
Insert comparison diagram here

Figure 2.1: Manual vs Cloud-Based Institute Management

2.3 Existing Systems and Their Limitations

Several management software products exist for educational institutions, including EduSys, Fedena, Classter, and various locally developed desktop applications. These solutions present significant limitations for small coaching institutes. Enterprise solutions carry high licensing fees beyond the budget of most centres. Desktop applications require installation on specific machines and cannot be accessed remotely. Generic school management systems include features designed for schools — timetabling, grade books, examination management — that are irrelevant for coaching centres and create unnecessary interface complexity. None of the reviewed solutions offer a Razorpay-integrated fee collection workflow with automatic receipt generation combined with a dues and defaulter tracking module in a single, affordable platform.

2.4 Cloud Computing Paradigm

Cloud computing represents a shift from traditional infrastructure ownership to on-demand consumption of compute, storage, and networking resources over the internet. The three primary service models — Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), Platform as a Service (PaaS), and Software as a Service (SaaS) — differ in the level of management responsibility delegated to the provider versus the customer. EduNova employs an IaaS model using Amazon EC2 for compute and Amazon DynamoDB as a managed NoSQL database service. This approach provides full control over the application runtime while delegating hardware management, network redundancy, and data replication to AWS. Amazon CloudFront distributes the frontend application from edge locations geographically close to users, reducing latency and improving page load times.

2.5 NoSQL Database Architecture

Traditional relational databases use predefined, rigid schemas and SQL for data retrieval. Amazon DynamoDB is a fully managed NoSQL key-value and document database offering single-digit millisecond read and write performance at any scale. EduNova uses DynamoDB with a single-table design pattern, where multiple entity types — students, payments, courses, users, and reminders — are stored in a single table differentiated by composite key patterns. Global Secondary Indexes (GSI) support cross-entity query patterns such as fetching all payments or all reminders without scanning the entire table.

CHAPTER 3
Need, Scope and Objectives of the Study

3.1 Need for the Study

Fee management is the most critical administrative process in any institute, yet it is managed manually by most centres. Handwritten receipts are easily lost and cannot be re-issued. Staff must manually calculate remaining balances, cross-reference installment schedules, and identify overdue students — tasks that consume significant time and introduce frequent errors. When a student raises a dispute about a payment, there is no reliable system to retrieve the historical transaction record.

Student lifecycle management is equally disorganized in manual systems. When a student takes a break, leaves without completing the course, or passes out after completion, these status changes are often informally noted without structured tracking. This makes it impossible to generate accurate counts of active students or identify which students are still liable for dues.

Financial reporting is largely non-existent in manual systems. Owners cannot easily determine their total monthly collection, the course-wise revenue breakdown, or how the current year's admissions compare to previous years without spending hours consolidating data from multiple registers.

3.2 Scope of the Study

The scope of EduNova covers the complete administrative and financial management cycle of an IT coaching institute. In terms of users, the system serves two roles: Admin, who has full access to all modules including user management, course setup, and financial reports; and Staff, who can access student records, process fee payments, and view dues.

The functional scope includes student admission and profile management, course and fee structure configuration, multi-mode fee collection with receipt generation, installment tracking, dues and defaulter management, reminder scheduling, clearance certification, and financial reporting with CSV export. The system is India-centric: fees are denominated in INR and the Razorpay integration supports UPI, card, and net-banking payment methods standard in the Indian market.

Aspects outside the current scope include GST billing and filing, accounting software integration, student attendance tracking, online class scheduling, and multi-branch management. These are identified as future scope items.

3.3 Objectives of the Study

  1. To design and implement a cloud-hosted institute management system that replaces manual, paper-based administrative workflows with a secure, centralised digital platform.
  2. To develop an automated fee installment scheduling system that generates monthly or yearly installment plans at the time of student admission based on the configured course fee structure.
  3. To implement a multi-mode fee collection module supporting Cash, Demand Draft, and Online (Razorpay) payment modes, with automatic printable receipt generation after each transaction.
  4. To build a real-time dues tracking system that identifies students with outstanding balances, calculates overdue durations, and automatically escalates chronic defaulters.
  5. To develop a reminder scheduling module enabling staff to set follow-up dates and promise amounts against individual students, with automatic escalation of overdue reminders.
  6. To generate financial reports including monthly collection trends, course-wise revenue analysis, and top defaulter lists, with CSV export capability.
  7. To implement role-based access control using JWT authentication to ensure only authorised users access sensitive modules.
  8. To integrate Twilio WhatsApp API for OTP-based password reset, providing a secure self-service mechanism for staff to recover account access.
  9. To deploy the system on AWS infrastructure and validate performance, security, and usability through comprehensive testing.
CHAPTER 4
Research Methodology

4.1 Development Methodology

The development of EduNova followed the Agile methodology with iterative sprints. Each sprint focused on a specific functional module: database design and API foundation, student management, course and fee structure configuration, fee collection and receipt generation, dues and defaulter tracking, reminders, reporting, and finally authentication and deployment. This approach enabled continuous testing and module-level validation before integration. The overall development progressed through five phases: Requirements Analysis and Architecture Design; AWS Infrastructure Setup; Backend API Development and DynamoDB Schema Design; Frontend SPA Development and API Integration; and Testing, Optimization, and Cloud Deployment.

4.2 System Architecture

EduNova follows a three-tier architecture separating the presentation, application, and data layers.

[ Figure 4.1 — Three-Tier Architecture Diagram ]
Insert architecture diagram here

Figure 4.1: Three-Tier Architecture Diagram

The Presentation Layer is a Single Page Application built with vanilla HTML5, CSS3, and JavaScript. All navigation occurs without page reloads using hash-based routing. The SPA is served from the EC2 server and distributed globally via Amazon CloudFront.

The Application Layer is a Python FastAPI application running as a systemd service on Amazon EC2 (t2.micro, ap-south-1). FastAPI provides automatic OpenAPI documentation, built-in data validation through Pydantic models, and asynchronous request handling via uvicorn ASGI server. The application is organized into route modules — auth, students, courses, payments, dues, defaulters, reminders, reports, clearance, dashboard, admin, and users — each registered as a separate APIRouter.

The Data Layer uses Amazon DynamoDB in a single-table design. All application entities are stored in a table named clerk_app and differentiated using composite primary keys (PK and SK). Global Secondary Indexes (GSI) support efficient cross-entity queries.

4.3 Database Design

Table 4.2: DynamoDB Single-Table Schema

EntityPKSKGSI1_PKGSI1_SK
StudentSTUDENT#{id}#METAALL#STUDENTSSTUDENT#{id}
PaymentSTUDENT#{id}PAYMENT#{id}ALL#PAYMENTS{date}#{id}
CourseCOURSE#{code}#METAALL#COURSESCOURSE#{code}
UserUSER#{username}#METAALL#USERSUSER#{username}
ReminderREMINDER#{id}#METAALL#REMINDERSREMINDER#{date}

Table 4.3: Student Record — Key Fields

FieldTypeDescription
idStringAuto-generated roll number (e.g., ST1001)
nameStringStudent full name
courseStringEnrolled course code
feeTypeStringmonthly or yearly
statusStringstudying / break / left / passout
totalFeesNumberTotal course fees after waiver deduction
paidNumberTotal amount paid to date
dueNumberRemaining outstanding balance
waiverAmountNumberFee concession amount granted
installmentsListArray of installment objects with due dates
admissionDateStringISO format date of admission
contactStringStudent contact number
fatherNameStringFather's full name
fatherMobileStringFather's contact number

Table 4.4: Payment Record — Key Fields

FieldTypeDescription
txnIdStringUnique transaction ID (e.g., TXN1001)
studentIdStringReference to associated student record
amountNumberAmount paid in this transaction (INR)
methodStringCash / DD / Online / Razorpay
dateStringPayment date in ISO format
displayTimeStringHuman-readable time (e.g., 02:30:00 PM)
statusStringPaid
onlineObjectRazorpay payment_id, order_id, signature

4.4 AWS Services Used

Table 4.1: AWS Services Used in EduNova

AWS ServicePurpose
Amazon EC2Virtual server hosting the FastAPI application
Amazon DynamoDBNoSQL database for all application data
Amazon CloudFrontCDN for global frontend delivery and caching
AWS IAMIdentity and access management for DynamoDB access

4.5 Security Architecture

All API endpoints, except the login, forgot-password, and OTP verification routes, require a valid JWT Bearer token in the Authorization header of every request. Tokens are issued on successful login with an 8-hour expiry and stored in the browser's sessionStorage, ensuring they are cleared when the tab is closed. Passwords are stored as bcrypt hashes and never returned in API responses. CORS middleware restricts cross-origin requests to the allowed frontend domain. The Razorpay secret key is stored exclusively in the server-side environment file and is never transmitted to the frontend; only the public key_id is returned via the /api/payments/razorpay-key endpoint.

[ Figure 4.2 — DynamoDB Entity Relationship Diagram ]
Insert ER diagram here

Figure 4.2: DynamoDB Entity Relationship Diagram

CHAPTER 5
Data Analysis and Interpretation

5.1 Student Data Structure Analysis

Each student record in DynamoDB maintains a complete financial history through an embedded installments array. At the time of admission, the system calculates the total course fee by multiplying the configured monthly or yearly fee by the course duration. The waiver amount, if any, is subtracted to arrive at the net total fees payable. The installment schedule is then auto-generated as a list of objects, each containing the installment number, amount due, due date, paid status, partial payment flag, amount paid to date, and the date on which payment was received. Every fee payment transaction updates the installments array in sequence, marking installments as fully paid or partially paid, and recalculates the aggregate paid and due fields on the student record.

5.2 Fee Collection Analysis

Three payment modes are supported. For Cash and Demand Draft payments, the staff enters the amount and payment date manually. The backend validates that the entered amount does not exceed the student's current outstanding balance and processes the transaction. For online payments, the frontend fetches the Razorpay public key from the backend, opens the Razorpay checkout window in the browser, and on successful payment, posts the transaction to the backend along with the Razorpay payment_id. The backend stores the Razorpay data as a nested object within the payment record for future reference. A professional receipt is generated in the browser after every payment, displaying the student name, roll number, father's name, course details, fee plan, amount paid in words and figures, payment history summary, remaining balance, and a visual payment progress bar. The receipt is formatted for A4 printing and can be saved as a PDF.

5.3 Dues and Defaulter Analysis

The dues module scans all student records and returns those with a non-zero due balance. For each student, the system identifies the next unpaid installment's due date and computes the isOverdue flag by comparing this date against the current system date. Students with status "left" or "passout" are excluded from the dues list as they are no longer active members. Results are sorted by outstanding balance in descending order to surface the most critical cases first.

The defaulters module extends this analysis by computing the number of months elapsed since admission for each student with outstanding dues. Students overdue by one or more months are included in the defaulters list. Those overdue by three or more months receive an isEscalated flag, which triggers a red visual indicator in the interface to prioritize follow-up. The contact number is returned alongside the defaulter record to facilitate immediate outreach.

5.4 Financial Reporting Analysis

The reports module aggregates data across all student and payment records to produce four categories of output: collection summaries covering this month, last month, all-time total collected, and total outstanding; monthly admission trends for the selected year rendered as a bar chart using Chart.js; course-wise breakdown showing the number of enrolled students and total fees collected per course; and a list of the top 20 serious defaulters with overdue duration and contact details. All report data is sourced from a single GET /api/reports endpoint, which performs all aggregation server-side and returns a consolidated JSON response.

5.5 Reminder Data Interpretation

Reminders are stored as independent records linked to a student ID. Each reminder captures the student name, course, current outstanding due at the time of creation, the scheduled follow-up date, a promise amount the student committed to, and a staff note. When the list of active reminders is fetched, the backend compares each reminder's scheduled date against the current date and automatically escalates overdue pending reminders by setting their status to "escalated". This happens transparently on every list fetch without requiring a separate background job or scheduler.

CHAPTER 6
Findings of the Study

6.1 Technical Findings

The FastAPI framework combined with the uvicorn ASGI server delivers API response times well under 500 milliseconds for all standard CRUD operations when data volume is in the range of 0 to 1,000 student records, which covers the typical scale of a small to medium coaching institute. DynamoDB's single-table design with GSI-based queries significantly reduces the number of read operations required for cross-entity queries. Fetching all payments across all students, for example, uses a single GSI query on ALL#PAYMENTS rather than multiple student-level queries.

Amazon CloudFront eliminates direct load on the EC2 server for all static assets (HTML, CSS, JS files). Only API calls reach the EC2 instance, substantially reducing bandwidth consumption and improving frontend load performance for users across different geographical locations. The Razorpay test mode integration successfully handles the complete payment lifecycle — key fetching, checkout popup, payment success callback, and backend recording — without requiring a production Razorpay account. Switching to live mode requires only updating the key credentials in the server-side environment configuration.

JWT authentication with sessionStorage token management provides an appropriate security level for an internal staff tool. The 8-hour token expiry ensures that sessions are not left open indefinitely, while the sessionStorage mechanism ensures tokens are cleared when the browser is closed.

6.2 Operational Findings

Based on functional testing with representative data sets, the system successfully automates the most time-consuming administrative tasks. Adding a new student and generating a complete installment schedule for a 3-year MCA course takes under 60 seconds including form filling. Collecting a fee payment — from opening the modal to printing the receipt — takes under 30 seconds. Generating the full monthly financial report takes under 2 seconds, compared to the estimated 2–3 hours required for manual calculation and consolidation.

6.3 System Limitations Identified

The current version does not support barcode or QR code scanning for student identification at fee collection time, which would further accelerate the payment workflow. The reporting module performs server-side aggregation over a full table scan, which may experience increased latency for very large datasets exceeding 5,000 students. GST-compliant invoice generation is not implemented, as the system is designed for a coaching centre context where GST applicability depends on annual turnover thresholds. Multi-branch deployment is not supported; each EC2 deployment serves a single institute location.

6.4 Comparison with Manual System

EduNova eliminates all manual fee record keeping, provides instant access to every student's complete payment history from any device, prevents arithmetic errors in balance calculation through automated computation, enables instant receipt reprinting for any historical transaction, and delivers real-time financial reports with zero manual effort. Student status tracking is formalized with four defined states — studying, break, left, passout — replacing informal register notes with structured, queryable data.

CHAPTER 7
Testing and Implementation

7.1 Testing Approach

Testing was conducted across four levels. Unit testing verified each API endpoint individually using the FastAPI built-in Swagger UI at /docs, providing an interactive interface for submitting test requests and inspecting responses. Integration testing verified the end-to-end workflow of key user journeys — student admission, fee collection, Razorpay payment, receipt generation, and report generation — across the full stack from browser to database. Security testing validated authentication enforcement, token expiry behaviour, CORS restrictions, and password storage security. User interface testing was performed on Google Chrome across desktop and mobile screen sizes.

[ Figure 7.1 — Dashboard Admin Panel Screenshot ]

[ Figure 7.2 — Fee Collection Modal — Razorpay Integration ]
Insert screenshot here

Figure 7.2: Fee Collection Modal — Razorpay Integration

7.3 Integration Test Scenarios

The student admission with installment auto-generation scenario was verified by adding a student to a 3-year MCA course configured at Rs. 4,000 per month, confirming that 36 installments were correctly generated with the first due date set to the admission date and subsequent dates incremented by one month. The fee collection workflow was verified for all three payment modes. Cash payment correctly updated the student's paid and due fields and generated the receipt. Razorpay payment successfully opened the checkout popup and, after simulating a test payment, the backend correctly recorded the transaction with the Razorpay payment_id stored in the online field. The CloudFront cache invalidation workflow was verified by modifying a frontend JavaScript file, uploading it to EC2, running an AWS CLI invalidation command, and confirming that the browser received the updated file on the next request.

7.4 Implementation Details

The application is deployed on an AWS EC2 t2.micro instance running Amazon Linux 2023 in the ap-south-1 (Mumbai) region. The FastAPI application is managed by a systemd service unit that starts the uvicorn server automatically on instance reboot, ensuring continuous availability without manual intervention. The DynamoDB table and GSI were created via the AWS Management Console. The CloudFront distribution is configured with the EC2 instance as the origin, with cache behaviours set to forward API calls (path /api/*) directly to the origin while caching all static assets at edge locations. Deployment of code changes is performed via SCP file transfer from the development machine to the EC2 instance, followed by a systemd service restart and a CloudFront invalidation for frontend asset changes.

CHAPTER 8
Conclusion, Suggestions & Recommendations

8.1 Conclusion

EduNova — Cloud-Based IT Coaching Institute Management System has been successfully designed, developed, deployed, and tested as a fully functional cloud-hosted web application. The system achieves all the objectives stated in Chapter 3 and directly addresses the operational challenges identified in Chapters 2 and 3. The project demonstrates practical application of technologies studied throughout the MCA programme, including web application development using REST API architecture, cloud computing with AWS, NoSQL database design with DynamoDB, payment gateway integration using Razorpay, and software security principles through JWT authentication and bcrypt password hashing.

The development process provided hands-on experience in single-table DynamoDB schema design, Python FastAPI application structure, Single Page Application development with vanilla JavaScript, and cloud deployment and configuration using AWS EC2, CloudFront, and DynamoDB.

8.2 Suggestions

The single-table DynamoDB design pattern significantly simplifies deployment and reduces operational overhead compared to multi-table designs, and is recommended for applications with a manageable number of entity types and predictable access patterns. JWT token management in sessionStorage provides a pragmatic balance between security and user experience for internal web tools; for higher security requirements, refresh token mechanisms with HTTP-only cookies should be considered. Integrating a payment gateway such as Razorpay early in the development cycle allows the fee collection data model to be designed with online payment metadata in mind from the outset, rather than retrofitting it as a later addition.

8.3 Recommendations for Future Enhancement

A dedicated mobile application for Android and iOS, developed using Flutter, would allow students to view their own fee status and download receipts without requiring access to the staff panel. Automated SMS and email notifications for upcoming installment due dates would reduce the need for manual follow-up by staff. A student self-service portal with separate login credentials would further reduce the administrative burden on institute staff.

Biometric attendance integration would allow the system to flag students absent beyond a defined threshold and cross-reference attendance with fee payment status. Multi-branch support, enabling a single deployment to manage multiple institute locations with branch-level data separation and a central management view, would broaden the applicability of the system considerably. Integration with accounting software such as Tally ERP through a CSV or API export of fee collection data would complete the financial management workflow for institute administrators. Government scholarship tracking, where scholarship amounts are recorded and automatically offset against fee dues, would be particularly valuable for institutes enrolling government-sponsored students.

  4. Amazon CloudFront Developer Guide. Amazon Web Services. (2024). https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cloudfront/
